channel.title: Drupal AI
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal AI.

item.title: #3578657: Drupal 10: Error on /admin/ai/context/items/add: scheduler_content_moderation_integration module conflict
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4446c84 · +184/-10 prod · +774/-46 test · +28/-9 infra · 15 files
  • Discussion: #3578657 · 5 contributors · 67 comments

Summary

A critical bug prevented the AI Context module from working immediately after installation on Drupal 10. When users tried to create their first AI Context item, the page crashed with a fatal error about missing scheduler integration fields. The error only occurred on Drupal 10, not Drupal 11, and required visiting a settings page first to trigger initialization. This fix ensures the required field definitions are created automatically before they are needed, making the module work correctly on fresh Drupal 10 installations without manual workarounds.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The issue was a timing race condition specific to Drupal 10. The ai_context_item/default bundle was already registered with the content moderation workflow, and Scheduler was enabled. When users visited /admin/ai/context/items/add immediately after installation, Scheduler Content Moderation Integration (SCMI) ran its form hooks before its publish_state and unpublish_state field storage definitions were materialized. This caused SCMI to dereference null when checking for those fields, triggering a fatal error.

The fix introduces AiSchedulerContentModerationSync, a dedicated class that lazily synchronizes SCMI field storage definitions before Scheduler's hooks run. The module now implements hook_scheduler_hide_publish_date() and hook_scheduler_hide_unpublish_date(), uses hook_module_implements_alter() to ensure these run first, and triggers the sync during hook_modules_installed() for any relevant module (ai_context, scheduler, or SCMI). The sync operation is idempotent and safe to call multiple times.

The fix only targets Drupal 10, where the issue was reproducible. Drupal 11 did not exhibit this problem. Test coverage was expanded to verify the add form works immediately after install with Scheduler enabled, and existing test workarounds that disabled Scheduler were removed.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 11, 2026
  • Committed: March 15, 2026 (3 days later)

Key contributors

  • dstorozhuk (iTech4web)
  • kristen pol (Itty Bitty Byte)
  • annmarysruthy (QED42)
  • scott falconer (Acquia)

Collaboration

This critical bug was reported March 11 and fixed by March 15, with an intense four-day sprint involving 67 comments and five contributors. The issue was discovered on a fresh installation and initially included detailed reproduction steps and AI-generated analysis.

Scott Falconer provided crucial debugging in comment #16, reproducing the issue on Drupal 10.6.5, identifying the exact failure state (SCMI fields not materialized), and confirming the workaround. He proposed the initial fix strategy and provided the core sync logic.

Dstorozhuk refactored the implementation into object-oriented style using AiSchedulerContentModerationSync, removed test workarounds, and worked to restore Drupal 10 to the CI matrix. He ran into challenging pipeline issues where tests failed locally but passed remotely (comment #62), which delayed final validation.

Kristen Pol managed the process, coordinated scope discussions to keep focus on the core fix, performed extensive AI-assisted code review, and tested manually on Drupal 10.5.8 to confirm the error and fix. Ann Mary helped with early verification on Drupal 11.

The collaboration showed strong teamwork under deadline pressure, with clear technical diagnosis, iterative refinement, and pragmatic scope management to ship the fix quickly. The team added TODO comments to remove the Drupal 10-specific workaround when support is eventually dropped.

Lessons learned

  • When a module's configuration entity type is marked as moderated and integrates with Scheduler, field storage for moderation integration fields (like publish_state and unpublish_state) must be materialized before any form_alter hooks attempt to access those fields. If lazy initialization is unavoidable, eagerly synchronize field storage definitions in your module's hook_form_alter implementation before delegating to third-party integrations, ensuring dependent modules don't dereference null field definitions. (via scott falconer)
  • When configuration is required for entity forms to render but isn't created during module installation, visiting the entity add form will fail. Ensure default configuration entities are created in hook_install or provide lazy initialization in form_alter before any hook that depends on that configuration runs, so users don't encounter errors on first use. (via scott falconer, dstorozhuk)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577427: Update context item revision diff feature for progressive enhancement
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 7890df5 · +227/-76 prod · +278/-34 test · +10/-1 infra · 20 files
  • Discussion: #3577427 · 7 contributors · 59 comments

Summary

The AI Context module now makes the Diff module optional for comparing revisions of context items. Previously, Diff was a required dependency even if revision comparison wasn't needed. The module now works without Diff installed, displaying a helpful message on the revisions page that invites users to install Diff if they want to enable the comparison feature. When Diff is enabled, the full revision comparison functionality appears automatically. This change reduces the installation footprint for sites that only need basic context item management without revision comparison capabilities.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Diff module was moved from require to require-dev in composer.json and added as a suggested dependency. The ai_context_item entity type now uses the core RevisionHtmlRouteProvider by default instead of always loading Diff's DiffRouteProvider. A new DiffHooks class implements hooks to conditionally swap in Diff's route provider and configure field settings only when the module is installed.

The revision overview form (AiContextItemRevisionOverviewForm) was updated to check whether Diff is enabled. When absent, it disables the radio buttons for selecting revisions, hides the compare button, and displays a status message linking to the Diff project page. When Diff is present, the full comparison UI appears as before.

Two new functional test classes cover both states: AiContextItemRevisionUiWithoutDiffTest confirms the revisions tab remains accessible and the install message appears, while AiContextItemRevisionUiWithDiffTest validates that the compare workflow reaches the diff route. The .gitlab-ci.yml file was updated to test against both Drupal 10.5 and 11.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 14, 2026 (8 days later)

Key contributors

  • kristen pol (Itty Bitty Byte)
  • a.dmitriiev (1xINTERNET)
  • scott falconer (Acquia)
  • erichomanchuk (Foster Interactive Inc.)
  • dstorozhuk (iTech4web)
  • danrod (OPTASY)

Collaboration

This issue took eight days from opening to commit with 59 comments and significant iteration. The initial approach by a.dmitriiev was simpler than expected but required multiple rounds of refinement. Scott Falconer identified regressions around duplicate revision tabs and missing routes, then contributed test coverage and a follow-up patch. Kristen pol refined the UX by updating status messages, disabling radio buttons when Diff is missing, and adding tooltips. Erichomanchuk caught a duplicate button placement issue and a separate Canvas module error unrelated to this change (filed separately as #3578668). A design discussion emerged between dstorozhuk and a.dmitriiev about whether to use services or class resolver for hook classes. The team ultimately chose the service approach for backward compatibility with Drupal 10 and to enable the hooks_converted: true performance optimization. Dstorozhuk documented the rationale in comment #58, providing helpful context for future contributors.

Lessons learned

  • When adding optional integrations with external modules, declare them as suggested dependencies rather than required dependencies, and conditionally load features only when the external module is present. Use service definitions and attribute-based discovery (e.g., route providers in entity annotations) to defer registration until runtime availability checks confirm the dependency is installed. (via a.dmitriiev, scott falconer)
  • When a framework feature can be dynamically provided by an optional dependency, provide graceful degradation in the UI: disable interactive controls, display informational messages explaining what capability is missing, and guide users toward enabling the optional module. This maintains core functionality while communicating upgrade paths. (via kristen pol)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577745: Context item revision comparison is missing some scope info
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 32c87c9 · +141/-4 prod · +587/-0 test · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3577745 · 5 contributors · 27 comments

Summary

When comparing revisions of AI context items, the Split Fields and Unified Fields diff views were missing scope plugin information. Only the Visual Inline view showed all changes. This made it difficult to track what changed between versions when using the more commonly used diff formats. The issue has been fixed so all three comparison formats now properly display scope changes with human-readable labels.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Diff module's CoreFieldBuilder handles map field types but checks for a value key in its build() method. Map items store data as key-value pairs without a value key, so the scope field was silently skipped in Split Fields and Unified Fields views.

The fix adds a new ScopeMapFieldDiffBuilder plugin specifically for the map field type used by the scope field. The plugin iterates over map key-value pairs and uses AiContextScopeManager to resolve human-readable labels for both scope types and their values. For example, it converts use_case: writing_words to "Use Cases: Writing Words".

The install file was updated to configure the scope field to use ai_context_scope_map_field_diff_builder instead of core_field_diff_builder. The MapFormatter was also updated to handle optional configuration keys with default values, preventing errors when used in diff contexts.

Five new kernel tests verify the plugin works with various scope configurations. A comprehensive functional test confirms that scope changes appear correctly in the revision comparison UI for all diff formats.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 7, 2026
  • Committed: March 14, 2026 (7 days later)

Key contributors

  • kristen pol (Itty Bitty Byte)
  • velmir_taky
  • hrishikesh-dalal

Collaboration

The issue was opened on March 7, 2026, as a follow-up to a progressive enhancement feature. A contributor identified the root cause within a day: the Diff module's CoreFieldBuilder couldn't process map fields because it checked for a 'value' key that map items don't have. They created a custom diff builder and comprehensive tests. Another contributor tested the code and discovered it needed configuration changes in the install file and suggested an update hook. The maintainer declined the update hook since the module hadn't reached beta1 yet, and requested a functional test in addition to the kernel tests. The contributor added the functional test, and the fix was committed on March 14. The rapid resolution came from clear problem diagnosis and collaborative iteration on the solution.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3522130: Add client-side transform for daterange_default to ReduxIntegratedFieldWidgetsHooks::fieldWidgetInfoAlter
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 0974302 · +61/-2 prod · +223/-14 test · +1/-0 infra · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3522130 · 5 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

When using date range fields in component forms, Canvas now properly transforms the field widget data between the editing interface and the stored values. Previously, attempting to use a date range field widget would cause a logic exception. This enables site builders to use date range fields in their components without errors, making more field types available for component configuration.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The issue was that daterange_default widget had no client-side transform mapping, causing failures when components used date range fields. The fix adds three pieces: a widget mapping in ReduxIntegratedFieldWidgetsHooks::fieldWidgetInfoAlter() pointing to a new dateRange transform, the transform itself in ui/src/utils/transforms.ts, and a corresponding canvas.transform.dateRange library definition.

The dateRange transform reuses the existing dateTime infrastructure. It extracts value and end_value from the form values, applies the dateTime transform to both using the field's datetime_type setting (date or datetime), and returns an object with both ISO-formatted values. The transform includes null/undefined guards aligned with other transforms.

Cypress E2E coverage was added to prop-types.cy.js enabling the datetime_range module and testing the daterange widget with autosave PATCH synchronization. The test handles both direct parameter and encoded JSON payload formats. Unit tests verify UTC/ISO mapping for both date and datetime range types. The change removes the previous test override and todo comment for #3523379.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 1, 2025
  • Committed: March 14, 2026 (10 months later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • scott falconer (Acquia)
  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)

Collaboration

This feature request took about 10 months from opening to commit. The initial issue identified a logic exception when enabling sdc_test_all_props due to missing date range transforms. Scott Falconer implemented a comprehensive solution including PHP widget mapping, TypeScript transforms, library registration, and extensive test coverage. Wim Leers requested extraction of out-of-scope Cypress stabilization changes to keep the MR focused, which Scott addressed by narrowing the scope back to daterange support only. Lauriii made the final commit. The thorough testing approach, including both unit and E2E coverage with payload format variations, demonstrates attention to production reliability.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3569776: Adopt AI Core shared UI library in CCC and escape data before rendering
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 330cb9e · +119/-57 · 13 files
  • Discussion: #3569776 · 7 contributors · 55 comments

Summary

The Context Control Center module now uses shared design components from the AI Core module, creating a more consistent user interface across AI-related modules. The update also fixes security issues by properly escaping user-generated text before displaying it on screen. Visual improvements include better tooltip behavior, clearer pill-style labels with improved color contrast, and refinements to how context scope information displays. The changes make the interface more accessible and reduce duplicate code between modules.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue migrated the Context Control Center (CCC) module from custom UI components to the shared CSS library provided by AI Core. The work included replacing custom tooltip implementation with the new AI Tooltip component using the native Popover API, adopting shared .ai-pill and .ai-description classes, and standardizing icon usage with AI Core's SVG library.

A security review identified XSS vulnerabilities where user input was rendered without escaping. The fix adds Html::escape() calls throughout AiContextScopeManager, form classes, and templates to sanitize labels, descriptions, and plugin names before rendering.

The pill component received accessibility improvements: the "light" variant now uses a light blue background with dark text to meet WCAG contrast requirements, replacing the previous light grey that failed accessibility checks. Pills are limited to 17 characters width with tooltips showing full text on longer values.

The external link icon switched from a Unicode character to an SVG mask from AI Core's shared assets, ensuring consistent rendering. CSS cleanup removed duplicate styles now handled by the shared library. The diff shows proper escaping in multiple locations including scope summaries, agent forms, and overview templates.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 27, 2026
  • First commit: March 10, 2026 (1 month later)
  • Last commit: March 14, 2026 (3 days later)

Key contributors

  • afoster (Foster Interactive Inc.)
  • kristen pol (Itty Bitty Byte)
  • bbruno (1xINTERNET)
  • erichomanchuk (Foster Interactive Inc.)
  • b_sharpe (ImageX)

Collaboration

This issue took a coordinated path across multiple modules and teams over seven weeks. Initially blocked waiting for the AI Core shared library to be ready (specifically tooltip functionality merged in #3569770), the work required careful sequencing with other major CCC changes including scope and subcontext UI updates.

Bruno implemented the initial migration to shared components, Kristen identified and fixed security issues flagged by AI code review including XSS risks and hardcoded asset paths, then refined the pill styling for accessibility. Eric conducted thorough review identifying CSS issues, whitespace problems in templates, and icon class inconsistencies. The team committed the main work in one merge request, then followed up with a second MR to remove left margin from the first pill in lists.

The blue pill color was debated but ultimately chosen for better visual distinction and accessibility compared to grey. Several issues were deferred to follow-ups including icon base class fixes that required changes in AI Core itself (#3579023). The tight timeline before a demo drove focused decision-making and pragmatic trade-offs between ideal architecture and shipping functional improvements.

Lessons learned

  • When rendering user-provided data in markup, escape all output to prevent XSS vulnerabilities. Use Drupal's render API with proper escaping mechanisms rather than raw #markup with unescaped strings. (via kristen pol)
  • When referencing assets from external modules, avoid hardcoded absolute paths. Use dynamic path resolution so the code remains portable across different installations and survives module reorganization. (via kristen pol)
  • When implementing UI updates that depend on unreleased upstream changes, document the dependency clearly and provide instructions for reviewers to test against the correct branch. This prevents confusion and enables parallel development. (via bbruno)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3574445: Add Drupal CMS 2.0 CCC install steps on the project page
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3574445 · 2 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

The AI Context module now includes installation instructions for Drupal CMS 2.0 on its project page. This helps users understand how to set up the module with the latest version of Drupal CMS. The instructions cover the complete installation process, including required dependencies from the AI module suite and configuration steps. This documentation update ensures that Drupal CMS 2.0 adopters can successfully integrate AI-powered contextual features into their sites.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue added documentation to the AI Context module's project page to support installation on Drupal CMS 2.0. The update was blocked until #3574420 added proper Drupal CMS 2.0 compatibility to the module itself.

The installation process documented requires several AI-related modules including ai:dev-1.3.x, ai_context:dev-1.0.x, and ai_agents_debugger. The instructions also include building the MDXEditor component via npm, which is part of the AI module suite.

The steps were tested from scratch using DDEV and include creating a new Drupal CMS project, unpacking recipes, adjusting composer stability settings to allow dev versions, and enabling the necessary modules. Configuration steps guide users through setting up an AI provider and testing agent context functionality.

This is purely a documentation change to the project page, with no code modifications to the module itself. The instructions help bridge the gap until dedicated documentation is created.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 19, 2026

Key contributors

  • kristen pol (Itty Bitty Byte)

Collaboration

This issue was postponed for several weeks while waiting for #3574420 to add actual Drupal CMS 2.0 support to the module. Kristen Pol handled both the technical testing and documentation updates, testing the complete installation workflow from scratch using a real AI provider. The issue moved from postponed to needs review once the blocking issue was resolved and the installation steps were verified to work correctly.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571925: Output streaming is handled inconsistently, fails to work in API AI-explorer
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3571925 · 4 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Output streaming for AI responses was failing in certain contexts like the API AI Explorer when using nginx web servers. The module was setting HTTP headers inconsistently across different integration points, missing critical headers like X-Accel-Buffering that control server-side buffering. This caused streamed responses to buffer completely before displaying, defeating the purpose of streaming. A new centralized response class now ensures all AI streaming responses use consistent headers and configuration, making streaming work reliably across all integration points regardless of web server setup.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The AI module had four different locations implementing streamed responses, each with different header configurations. Some set X-Accel-Buffering: no (required for nginx), others did not. Cache-Control and Content-Type headers varied, and output buffer handling was inconsistent. The ai_ckeditor integration worked because it set the nginx header, while ai_api_explorer and some ai_chatbot contexts failed.

The fix introduces a centralized AiStreamedResponse class extending Symfony's StreamedResponse. This class sets all required headers by default: X-Accel-Buffering: no for nginx compatibility, Cache-Control: no-cache to prevent caching, and appropriate Content-Type headers. It also handles output buffer cleanup with ob_end_clean. All four streaming locations now use this single class, ensuring consistent behavior.

The Content-Type handling was also corrected. CKEditor was incorrectly using text/event-stream without actually implementing Server-Sent Events, while DeepChat was sending SSE data without the proper header. The standardized class resolves these inconsistencies.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026

Key contributors

  • fago (drunomics)
  • petar_basic (drunomics)

Collaboration

This issue was opened and resolved by fago, who identified the problem in an nginx environment where API Explorer streaming failed while CKEditor worked. The investigation revealed inconsistent header handling across four different streaming implementations in the AI module. After creating a merge request with a centralized solution, petar_basic tested with a non-agent chatbot configuration and confirmed the fix worked correctly. The issue moved quickly from problem identification to solution, with both contributors from drunomics working together to verify the fix across different AI integration points.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3574420: Add Drupal CMS 2.0 support to CCC
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 32198d8 · +1/-1 prod · +2/-2 infra · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3574420 · 6 contributors · 41 comments

Summary

The Context Control Center module now works with Drupal CMS 2.0. Previously, installing the module failed due to conflicting version requirements for the ECA module. The dependency conflict has been resolved by removing the ECA dependency and updating version constraints to support both Drupal 10.5+ and Drupal 11.2+. This enables site owners using Drupal CMS 2.0 to install and use the Context Control Center to manage AI context used in agent prompts.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The issue originated from a version conflict where the module required drupal/eca ^2.1, but Drupal CMS 2.0 required drupal/eca ^3.0.8. The upstream AI module (1.3.x) removed its ECA dependency, which allowed this module to do the same via #3577426.

The module's composer.json was updated to broaden compatibility. The drupal/core requirement changed from ^11.2 to ^10.5 || ^11.2, adding Drupal 10.5+ support. The drupal/ai requirement changed from ^1.3 to >=1.3.0-rc2@RC || ^2 to ensure compatibility with AI Core RC2+ (needed for styling and icon work) while also allowing version 2.x.

The ai_context.info.yml file was updated to reflect the new core version requirement: ^10.5 || ^11.2 instead of ^10.4 || ^11. This change ensures the module properly declares its compatibility with both Drupal 10.5+ and 11.2+, enabling installation on Drupal CMS 2.0 environments.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 19, 2026
  • Committed: March 8, 2026 (16 days later)

Key contributors

  • kristen pol (Itty Bitty Byte)
  • divyamdotfoo
  • kostiantyn (iTech4web)
  • axioteo (E-Sepia Web Innovation)
  • hrishikesh-dalal

Collaboration

This issue progressed through several stages over three weeks. Initially blocked by an ECA version conflict, the path forward became clear when the upstream AI module removed its ECA dependency. A parallel effort in #3577426 removed the ECA dependency from this module by reimplementing the context item duplicate feature without ECA, which was completed ahead of schedule by kostiantyn.

Multiple contributors tested the installation process on Drupal CMS 2.0. divyamdotfoo provided detailed testing feedback via screencast showing the installation steps. After the dependency changes merged, kristen pol developed and verified complete installation instructions for Drupal CMS 2.0. hrishikesh-dalal provided final testing confirmation with screenshot evidence, leading to RTBC status.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576225: Index Strategy for the Drupal-created collections
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c4933a3 · +571/-67 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3576225 · 4 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

The AI VDB Provider Postgres module now allows you to choose how your vector search data is indexed. You can select between exact search for small datasets or use HNSW or IVFFlat strategies for faster searches on large datasets. This setting is exposed in the Search API server configuration form and can be changed at any time. The module automatically creates or updates the database index when you save your configuration, and it respects your chosen similarity metric (cosine, euclidean, or inner product) when building the index.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New VectorIndexStrategy enum and PostgresPgvectorClient::ensureVectorIndex() method for managing pgvector index strategies

Technical details

The PostgresProvider plugin now exposes a Vector index strategy select element in buildSettingsForm() with three options: None (exact search via sequential scan), HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World), and IVFFlat. The selected strategy is persisted in database_settings.vector_index_strategy within the Search API server configuration.

Because the upstream ai_search module does not invoke provider submitSettingsForm() during normal form submission, index application happens via a ConfigEvents::SAVE event subscriber at priority -10. This runs after the upstream subscriber (priority 0) that creates the collection table, ensuring the table exists before index operations.

The PostgresPgvectorClient::ensureVectorIndex() method compares the current index state (strategy and operator class) against the desired configuration and only drops and recreates the index when they differ. The operator class is derived from the configured similarity metric: vector_cosine_ops, vector_l2_ops, or vector_ip_ops.

A new VectorIndexStrategy backed enum provides case definitions, human-readable labels, and form options. VectorIndexException is thrown on index operation failures and logged without failing the entire config save. Index changes are also reapplied after clearing all indexed data via deleteAllItems().

Upgrade

No external code changes are required. This is a new feature exposed through the UI.

If you maintain custom code that directly calls PostgresPgvectorClient methods or extends PostgresProvider, note that the client now provides public methods ensureVectorIndex(), createVectorIndex(), dropVectorIndex(), and getVectorIndexStrategy() for managing pgvector indexes programmatically. The new VectorIndexStrategy enum is available at \Drupal\ai_vdb_provider_postgres\Enum\VectorIndexStrategy.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 27, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (7 days later)

Key contributors

  • nikro (Dropsolid)
  • abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid)

Collaboration

This feature request was opened and implemented by nikro (Dropsolid) with testing and review by abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid). The issue was opened on February 27, 2026 and committed on March 6, 2026, a turnaround of about one week.

The initial patch was comprehensive, including the enum, event subscriber, client methods, form integration, and automatic reapplication after clearing data. abhisekmazumdar noted they had delayed review while setting up the required pgvector environment, but once tested the feature worked as designed across all transitions (None to HNSW, HNSW to IVFFlat, etc.) and correctly applied metric-aware operator classes.

The diff included PHPCS cleanup of pre-existing code style issues (unqualified type hints, trailing commas, docblock formatting), which nikro noted upfront in comment #4. The issue summary tracked completion of six planned tasks; the last two (automated tests and documentation updates) were skipped. Tests were deemed impractical due to the need for a live pgvector connection in CI, and documentation was considered low priority.

Lessons learned

  • When a framework creates resources (tables, indexes) in response to configuration saves but does not expose a submit handler, use a config save event subscriber at a lower priority than the framework's own subscriber. This ensures the framework's creation logic completes before your dependent logic (like index creation) runs, guaranteeing the resource exists before you reference it. (via nikro)
  • When managing database indexes based on user configuration, detect the current index state before making changes. Drop and recreate only when the desired strategy or operator class differs from what exists, avoiding unnecessary schema operations that can be expensive on large tables. (via nikro)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3507378: Use HEREDOC style string for AI API Explorer
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 2.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ec8b39a · +207/-141 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3507378 · 5 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

The AI API Explorer submodule now uses PHP heredoc syntax for generating code examples. This makes the example code easier to read and maintain. When developers explore different AI operations like chat, embeddings, or text-to-image generation, they see cleaner code snippets that demonstrate how to use the API. The change only affects how the code examples are constructed internally and does not change the functionality or output of the explorer.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The AI API Explorer generates code examples to help developers learn how to use different AI operations. Previously, these examples were built by concatenating many short strings with the .= operator, each adding a line of code. This resulted in long, hard-to-read methods where variables had to be manually escaped and the flow was difficult to follow.

The refactoring changes all code example generation methods across seven plugin files (ChatGenerator, EmbeddingsGenerator, ModerationGenerator, SpeechToTextGenerator, TextToImageGenerator, and TextToSpeechGenerator) to use PHP heredoc syntax. With heredoc, multi-line code blocks are written as literal text between <<<END and END markers, with variables interpolated directly.

This approach extracts form values into variables at the start of each method, then uses those variables within the heredoc blocks. The resulting code is more readable and maintainable because the PHP code closely resembles the actual example code being generated. Variable escaping is simplified since heredoc handles it naturally.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 18, 2025
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (1 year later)

Key contributors

  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • alxdemi
  • robloach (Kalamuna)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3573054: Create Document Loader Plugin
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major feature request
  • Version: 1.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f28f169 · +1075/-430 prod · +493/-0 test · +282/-38 infra · 23 files
  • Discussion: #3573054 · 5 contributors · 27 comments

Summary

The AI File to Text module now provides a Document Loader plugin that allows other modules to convert documents into text, HTML, or Markdown without knowing which underlying service is being used. This makes it easier to build portable AI agents and automators that work with documents. The module ships with support for Word, PDF, spreadsheets, plain text, and Markdown files out of the box. Other modules can add support for new file types by registering extractor services without modifying the core module.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New document_loader:file plugin for document conversion; extractors can be registered via ai_file_to_text.extractor service tag.

Technical details

The module now integrates with the Document Loader plugin system. A single FileDocumentLoader plugin serves as the entry point for all file conversions, delegating to the existing FileExtractorManager layer. Each extractor now declares its document_loader_type (e.g. document_loader_type:pdf) and supported output formats via getDocumentLoaderType() and getSupportedOutputFormats() interface methods.

The FileExtractorManager collects all extractors tagged with ai_file_to_text.extractor, builds an extension-to-extractor map, and aggregates document loader types and output formats. A hook_document_loader_info_alter() implementation patches the cached plugin definition so getLoaderByType() can discover the plugin for any type, including types added by third-party modules.

When extractors have different output capabilities (e.g. PDF supports HTML but PowerPoint supports only text), the alter hook splits the plugin definition into multiple entries, each with accurate output_types. This prevents false positives when filtering by output format. The FileExtractorManager validates per-extension format support and falls back to plain text when a requested format is unsupported.

The existing AI automators and function calls now use the unified Document Loader plugin instead of calling extractors directly. The CsvExtractor and OdsExtractor classes were merged into a single SpreadsheetExtractor using PHPOffice/PhpSpreadsheet for all tabular formats.

Upgrade

To use the Document Loader plugin in custom code, create a FileInput object and call load():

use Drupal\ai_file_to_text\DocumentLoaderType\Input\FileInput;

$input = new FileInput('public://docs/report.pdf', 'markdown');
$loader = \Drupal::service('plugin.manager.document_loader')
  ->createInstance('document_loader:file');
$output = $loader->load($input);
$markdown = $output->getContent();

Or discover by type:

$loader = \Drupal::service('plugin.manager.document_loader')
  ->getLoaderByType('document_loader_type:spreadsheet', 'html');

To add support for a new file type, implement ExtractorInterface with methods isAvailable(), getDocumentLoaderType(), getSupportedOutputFormats(), getSupportedExtensions(), extractText(), and extractHtml(). Register it as a service tagged with ai_file_to_text.extractor:

services:
  your_module.extractor.pptx:
    class: Drupal\your_module\Extractor\PptxExtractor
    tags:
      - { name: ai_file_to_text.extractor }

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 12, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (20 days later)

Key contributors

  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • unqunq (DigitalTrotter)
  • ahmad khader (Vardot)
  • robloach (Kalamuna)

Collaboration

This feature was proposed by marcus_johansson in February 2026 to reduce overlap with the Unstructured module and enable portable AI workflows. unqunq created the first implementation (MR !1) splitting functionality into three separate plugins. ahmad khader then proposed a unified architecture in a new branch with a single FileDocumentLoader plugin that dynamically discovers types from tagged extractor services. After several iterations addressing UI feedback from robloach and refining the capability grouping logic to prevent false positives in type lookups, the unified approach was committed. The collaboration demonstrates the value of trying multiple approaches before settling on the simplest extensible design.

Lessons learned

  • When building plugin systems for file processing, use a manager to dispatch to format-specific handlers rather than creating separate plugins for each file type. Register each handler as a tagged service that declares its supported types, and let the manager collect all types at construction time to automatically expose them through a single unified plugin. This allows third-party modules to add new file format support without modifying the core plugin system. (via ahmad khader)
  • When abstracting functionality across multiple implementations, use a plugin type system that lets consumers request capabilities generically (e.g., 'any document loader that handles DocX to Markdown') without specifying which implementation to use. This enables portable configuration and recipes that work regardless of which specific implementation is installed. (via marcus_johansson)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576537: FAQ Field FWA should fetch multiple
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: de8ed5c · +192/-92 · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3576537 · 4 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

The FAQ Field integration with AI Automators previously created a separate button for each FAQ item in a multi-value field. This meant users had to click multiple times and trigger separate AI inference jobs for each item, even though they wanted to generate all FAQs at once from the same source text. The field widget action now places a single button that generates all FAQ items in one operation, populating the entire field with multiple questions and answers from a single AI call.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The FaqField plugin was treating the FAQ field as if each item needed separate processing. The fix adds multiple: FALSE to the plugin annotation to indicate the button should wrap the entire field rather than each delta.

The plugin now implements a dedicated runAutomatorSubmit() submit handler that runs before the AJAX callback. This handler runs the automator once for the entire field, counts the returned FAQ items, and updates the form state accordingly. It manipulates the user input array to include all generated items and uses WidgetBase::getWidgetState() and WidgetBase::setWidgetState() to update the items_count, ensuring the form rebuilds with enough delta slots to display all generated FAQs.

The aiAutomatorsAjax() method was simplified to just return the field element after clearing the rebuild flag. The old saveFormValues() method that manually set form element values was replaced with the state-based approach, which properly integrates with Drupal's form API and ensures the cached form structure matches the actual data.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 2, 2026
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (2 days later)

Key contributors

  • harivansh (OpenSense Labs)
  • a.dmitriiev (1xINTERNET)

Collaboration

The issue was resolved quickly, moving from report to commit in two days. The reporter provided detailed reproduction steps with 33 numbered instructions. A contributor identified that the same pattern affected the address module and suggested fixing it at the Field Widget Actions level rather than in individual plugins. After implementation, another contributor provided RTBC with screenshot verification showing the single-button behavior working correctly.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567244: Make it possible to serialize ChatMessage when an image exists
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.2.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 476cb97 · +44/-8 prod · +285/-108 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3567244 · 3 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

The AI module now correctly serializes chat messages that contain images. Previously, attempting to convert a ChatInput with attached images to an array and back would fail because the system tried to store image data as raw binary. This made it impossible to cache, queue, or otherwise persist conversations that included images. The fix switches to base64 encoding for image serialization, enabling round-trip conversion while preserving all image data including MIME type and filename.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The AbstractFileBase::toArray() method previously stored image binary data directly under a binary key. This caused serialization failures when ChatMessage objects with attached images were converted to arrays for storage or transmission.

The fix changes the serialization format to use base64 encoding instead. The toArray() method now stores images under a base64 key using the existing getAsBase64EncodedString() method, which generates data URL format strings like data:image/png;base64,<encoded_data>.

A new setFromBase64EncodedString() method in GenerateBase64Trait handles deserialization. It extracts the MIME type from data URL schemes if present, strips the prefix, and decodes the base64 string back to binary. The fromArray() method now calls this new method instead of setBinary().

The ChatMessage::toArray() method was updated to call toArray() on each file object rather than getBinary(), ensuring the base64 serialization format is used throughout the stack.

Five comprehensive unit tests verify round-trip serialization for single images, multiple images, and complete ChatInput objects with image messages.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2026
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid)

Collaboration

The issue progressed smoothly from report to resolution over seven weeks. Marcus Johansson identified the binary serialization problem in mid-January and proposed switching to base64 encoding. Abhisek Mazumdar tested the initial patch using the AI API Explorer Chat Generator with PNG uploads, verifying that images serialize correctly and round-trip data is preserved. He suggested adding error handling for invalid base64 strings in the deserialization method, though this was marked optional. Marcus delayed the merge initially to allow time for more extensive testing before a scheduled release, then committed the change in early March after the testing window.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3569242: Support Rerank Operation (not yet released)
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5db31b0 · +82/-5 prod · +8/-1 infra · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3569242 · 4 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

The AI LiteLLM provider module now supports the rerank operation, which lets AI models rearrange search results or documents based on their relevance to a query. Reranking is a popular AI capability that helps improve search quality by using machine learning to score and reorder results. This feature works with AI providers like Together AI that offer reranking models through the LiteLLM proxy. The implementation demonstrates the new rerank operation introduced in the parent AI module.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change adds rerank operation support to the AI LiteLLM provider module by implementing the ReRankInterface from the AI module. The implementation adds a rerank() method that sends requests to the LiteLLM proxy's /rerank endpoint, which follows Cohere's rerank API structure.

The Model DTO was updated to include a $supportsRerank property that checks if the model's mode is "rerank". The getModels() method now filters models for the "rerank" operation type, checking both the capability flag and whether the model name contains "rerank" to handle cases where capabilities may not be correctly reported.

The rerank payload includes the model ID, query string, input documents, and optional top_n parameter to limit results. The API response is mapped into a ReRankOutput object containing reranked documents with scores and metadata. If the API only returns indices, the original document text is re-hydrated from the input.

The module's dependency was updated from AI module 1.2.0 to 1.3.0-rc2, which includes the base rerank operation support. Project-specific spelling words were added for code quality checks.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 23, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • nikro (Dropsolid)
  • abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid)
  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)

Collaboration

This feature was developed in parallel with the parent AI module's rerank operation foundation (#3488114) and explorer interface (#3569232). The issue author nikro created a working draft locally before opening the issue, then provided detailed setup instructions including Docker commands for running a local LiteLLM proxy and configuration examples for Together AI's reranking models.

The review process was straightforward. Abhisekmazumdar tested the feature following the comprehensive setup steps, confirmed functionality with a screenshot demonstration, and moved it to RTBC (Reviewed and Tested by the Community) with only minor inline suggestions. The change was held from merging until the parent rerank operation issue was committed, ensuring proper dependency coordination across the AI module ecosystem.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575363: Create object detection validation
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a8ed233 · +477/-0 prod · +14/-10 infra · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3575363 · 3 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

The AI Validations module now supports validating uploaded images based on what objects appear in them. You can configure validation rules to require or forbid specific objects like "person," "backpack," or "apple" in images. This uses object detection AI models that run quickly, often in milliseconds, and can work on standard webserver hardware or free services like Huggingface. You can combine multiple object requirements with AND or OR logic, set minimum confidence thresholds, and choose whether validation passes when objects are found or when they are missing. This gives site owners fast, automated content moderation based on image content.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This feature adds a fourth Field Validation plugin to the AI Validations module: AiObjectDetectionConstraintFieldValidationRule. It integrates with the AI module's object detection operation introduced in #3570609.

The plugin provides a configuration form where administrators select an AI provider and model supporting object detection, enter keywords for object labels to detect, choose a matching strategy (exact, contains, or case-insensitive substring), and set a minimum confidence threshold. The keyword filter supports AND (all keywords must match) or OR (any keyword must match) logic. The rule mode determines whether validation passes or fails when objects are found.

The validator (AiObjectDetectionConstraintValidator) loads the uploaded file, creates an ObjectDetectionInput with the image, and calls the AI provider's objectDetection() method. It filters detections by confidence score, matches labels against configured keywords using the selected finder strategy, and applies the keyword filter logic. Based on the rule mode (approve or disapprove when found), it adds a constraint violation if the condition is met. When the model is unavailable, the validator can skip validation or fail based on configuration.

The composer.json now requires drupal/ai version ^1.3.x-dev to ensure object detection support is available.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 24, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (2 days later)

Key contributors

  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid)

Collaboration

This feature was delivered quickly, moving from issue creation to commit in just two days. The issue was opened on February 24, 2026, and committed on February 26, 2026. The collaboration between marcus_johansson and abhisekmazumdar was efficient and focused. abhisekmazumdar implemented the feature and provided detailed testing instructions including setup steps, configuration options, and a screenshot. marcus_johansson reviewed the work and identified a missing dependency issue where the composer.json needed to require AI 1.3.x-dev. abhisekmazumdar quickly added the fix, and the merge followed. A follow-up documentation issue #3576079 was created post-commit. The contributor noted that AI-generated code was used with human guidance, review, testing, and refinement.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3545462: Move the input of structured output to an object.
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 7d256a9 · +728/-28 prod · +266/-6 test · +62/-0 infra · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3545462 · 10 contributors · 57 comments

Summary

The AI module now provides a dedicated data transfer object (DTO) to help developers request structured JSON responses from AI providers. Previously, developers passed raw arrays to define the shape of AI responses. This worked but had no validation and different providers required different formats. The new DTO validates schema names, checks that required fields are present, and normalizes the array format for provider compatibility. Developers can still pass plain arrays for backwards compatibility, but the DTO approach is now recommended for cleaner code and better error messages. This lays the groundwork for future features like automated schema generation from PHP classes.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: ChatInput::setChatStrictSchema() and getChatStrictSchema() deprecated in ai:1.3.0, removed in ai:2.0.0; set strict on StructuredOutputSchema instead
  • New API: New StructuredOutputSchema DTO class for structured output requests (recommended; plain arrays still accepted for backwards compatibility)

Technical details

The new StructuredOutputSchema class provides a validated wrapper for structured output requests. It accepts four properties: name (validated as lowercase letters, numbers, underscores, hyphens only), description (optional), strict (boolean, default false), and json_schema (the actual JSON schema array, must contain a 'properties' key). The class uses Symfony Validator constraints on public properties to validate inputs at construction or when set via setters.

The DTO can be instantiated directly or created from an array via fromArray(). The toArray() method returns a normalized array with the key 'schema' for the schema content, matching the documented format most providers expect. For backwards compatibility, both the DTO and ChatInput::setChatStructuredJsonSchema() accept either 'schema' or 'json_schema' as the key for the schema content, but normalize and store it as 'schema'.

When a StructuredOutputSchema object is passed to ChatInput::setChatStructuredJsonSchema(), it is converted to the normalized array format and stored. For backwards compatibility, the strict flag is also copied to the now-deprecated chatStrictSchema property. Plain arrays are still accepted and are normalized to the same format. The API Explorer was updated to use the new DTO when processing JSON schema form input.

Upgrade

For module developers using structured output:

The recommended approach is now to use the StructuredOutputSchema DTO:

use Drupal\ai\Dto\StructuredOutputSchema;

$schema = new StructuredOutputSchema(
  name: 'weather_response',
  description: 'Structured weather data',
  strict: TRUE,
  json_schema: [
    'properties' => [
      'temperature' => ['type' => 'number'],
      'location' => ['type' => 'string'],
    ],
  ],
);

$input->setChatStructuredJsonSchema($schema);

Plain arrays still work but will be normalized (the 'json_schema' key is accepted but stored as 'schema'):

$input->setChatStructuredJsonSchema([
  'name' => 'my_schema',
  'strict' => TRUE,
  'schema' => [
    'properties' => ['field' => ['type' => 'string']],
  ],
]);

Replace calls to setChatStrictSchema() and getChatStrictSchema() with the strict property on the schema object or array (these methods are deprecated in ai:1.3.0 and removed in ai:2.0.0).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 8, 2025
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (5 months later)

Collaboration

abhisekmazumdar led the implementation work, iterating through multiple rounds of feedback to address backwards compatibility concerns, schema key normalization, and documentation. b_sharpe performed a thorough review and managed a complex merge request cleanup when the fork tracking broke. a.dmitriiev contributed rebase work and caught deprecation message issues. marcus_johansson identified a critical regression related to the schema key format during testing and guided the normalization strategy to maintain provider compatibility. The issue surfaced important architectural discussions about validation scope and provider abstraction.

Lessons learned

  • When building an abstraction layer for multiple providers, accept both structured objects and raw arrays at the API boundary, then normalize to a standard format internally. This allows gradual migration to the structured approach while maintaining backward compatibility with existing array-based implementations. (via marcus_johansson, abhisekmazumdar)
  • When different providers require different input formats for the same concept, create a translation layer in each provider module rather than exposing provider-specific variations in the shared API. The abstraction should enforce a common interface that works across providers, allowing users to switch providers without code changes. (via marcus_johansson)
  • When introducing structured DTOs to replace arrays, support multiple key names (like 'schema' and 'json_schema') during the transition period. Normalize to a canonical key internally while accepting legacy formats on input to prevent breaking production code. (via abhisekmazumdar)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3569770: Add tooltip component to AI Module for use in AI Dashboard using native Popover API
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 7bf7abc · +190/-4 prod · +5/-0 infra · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3569770 · 5 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

The AI module now includes a reusable tooltip component built on the native HTML Popover API with CSS Anchor Positioning. Tooltips automatically reposition themselves when near screen edges, improving usability on smaller viewports. The first implementation appears in the AI settings form, where information icons now display helpful tooltips on hover or keyboard focus instead of basic browser tooltips. This provides a better user experience with consistent styling and intelligent positioning across the module.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The tooltip component uses the HTML Popover API with popover="hint" and CSS Anchor Positioning with position-try-fallbacks: flip-inline to automatically flip tooltips when they would extend beyond the viewport edge. The implementation lives in ai_global.js as a Drupal behavior that finds elements with a data-ai-tooltip attribute and creates associated popover elements.

The JavaScript handles delayed showing (500ms) and immediate hiding with timer management to prevent race conditions. It attaches both mouse and keyboard event handlers for accessibility. The ai_global library is now attached globally via hook_page_attachments() instead of only on forms, enabling tooltips anywhere in the module. All classes use the ai- prefix to avoid conflicts.

The native Popover API is supported by all major browsers since 2025. The hint value provides additional tooltip priority features but degrades gracefully in browsers that don't support it. No polyfill was added since core functionality works universally. The CSS uses Gin admin theme variables for consistent styling with borders, shadows, and proper spacing.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 27, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

bbruno implemented the tooltip component and provided thorough testing instructions with a visual demonstration. ahmad khader performed comprehensive manual testing across multiple scenarios including viewport positioning, keyboard navigation, RTL support, and accessibility attributes, providing detailed verification of each requirement.

Lessons learned

  • When adding UI components that may be reused across a module, use data attributes (e.g., data-*) to declaratively attach functionality to elements. This separates configuration from implementation and allows developers to add features like tooltips without writing JavaScript, making the component API simple and intuitive for other developers to adopt. (via bbruno, ahmad khader)
  • When building tooltip or popover components, leverage native browser APIs (like the Popover API) even when optional features (like popover='hint') aren't universally supported. If the core functionality works across all major browsers and the unsupported parts provide only minor enhancements (like tooltip priority), the simplicity of avoiding polyfills outweighs the loss of those features. (via bbruno)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3518356: Add Full screen and Expand options to Deepchat chatbot
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 2.0.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 16f7a56 · +500/-24 · 20 files
  • Discussion: #3518356 · 12 contributors · 36 comments

Summary

The AI Chatbot block now offers configurable expansion modes to improve readability when the chatbot is placed in the toolbar. Administrators can choose between three options: none, expand (widens the sidebar and shifts page content), or fullscreen (opens as a modal overlay). This addresses usability concerns with the default sidebar's limited space, giving users more room to interact with longer conversations. The feature includes smooth animations, keyboard support for exiting fullscreen with the Escape key, and conditional loading of assets to avoid unnecessary overhead.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

When the AI Chatbot block is configured with toolbar placement, a new "Expansion method" select field appears in the block settings under styling options. The field offers three values: "none", "expand", and "fullscreen". The expand mode uses CSS custom properties (--toolbar-chatbot-expand-amount) to dynamically calculate width adjustments, pushing Drupal's displacement offset and page content to the right by 20rem. The fullscreen mode applies a fade-in animation and renders the chatbot as a modal using pure CSS (fixed positioning with a backdrop overlay), preserving the chatbot's current state without creating a new dialog element. The fullscreen library (toolbar-fullscreen.css) is conditionally attached only when the fullscreen method is selected. JavaScript in toolbar-chatbot.js handles expand button clicks, toggling body classes (ai-chatbot-expanded, ai-chatbot-fullscreen), managing aria-expanded attributes for accessibility, and listening for the Escape key to exit fullscreen. Exit animations use animationend events with double requestAnimationFrame to ensure clean state transitions. Both expansion states reset properly when the chatbot is closed. The expand button is omitted from the DOM when set to "none". SVG icons are updated to use currentColor for theme consistency.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 10, 2025
  • First commit: February 19, 2026 (10 months later)
  • Last commit: February 26, 2026 (6 days later)

Key contributors

  • bbruno (1xINTERNET)
  • mrdalesmith (FreelyGive)
  • kristen pol (Salsa Digital)
  • rajeshreeputra (Acquia)
  • venkatesh rajan.j
  • unqunq (DigitalTrotter)
  • petar_basic (drunomics)
  • ahmad khader
  • davidbp

Collaboration

bbruno implemented the feature, including the block configuration, CSS architecture, JavaScript state management, and accessibility support. petar_basic provided two rounds of review with detailed suggestions that improved code quality. ahmad khader conducted thorough testing across multiple scenarios, identified edge cases around state cleanup, and provided actionable feedback on code consistency and accessibility attributes. rajeshreeputra and davidbp made their first commits to the issue fork, contributing to the collaborative effort.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575631: Add a summarize automator
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e44d484 · +149/-0 prod · +76/-0 infra · 7 files
  • Discussion: #3575631 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

The AI module now includes a summarize automator that can automatically generate concise summaries from longer text content. This builds on the summarization operation type added previously by making it accessible for actual use. Site builders can configure fields to automatically generate summaries from other fields, such as creating article teasers from body content or meta descriptions from page text. The automator uses dedicated AI summarization models, which are optimized for extracting key information rather than using chat-based language models.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue implements two new automator plugins for the AI Automators module: LlmSummarizeToStringLong for plain text fields and LlmSummarizeToTextLong for formatted text fields. Both extend a new Summarize base class that handles the core summarization logic.

The Summarize base class sets the LLM type to "summarize" and implements the generate() method to iterate through base field items, creating SummarizationInput objects and calling the AI provider's summarize() method. Unlike chat-based automators, summarization does not require prompts or support advanced mode configuration.

The LlmSummarizeToTextLong variant adds an extra configuration option for selecting a text format, ensuring the generated summary maintains proper formatting. This is important for cron jobs that run anonymously.

The implementation works with AI summarization providers such as Hugging Face models (BART, DistilBART, Pegasus) and Google Chrome's built-in summarization API. Documentation was added covering field types, requirements, and eight common use cases including article summaries, teaser generation, and meta descriptions.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026

Key contributors

  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid)

Collaboration

This feature was delivered rapidly, moving from issue creation to commit in just one day. The implementation built directly on the summarization operation type added in #3574178. One contributor asked about extending support to text_with_summary field types, noting this could be addressed in a future issue. The documentation files included in the patch were marked as AI-generated, demonstrating practical use of AI tools in the module's own development.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575626: Unsupported parameter: 'top_p' is not supported with this model.
item.description:
• Project: Drupal AI
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e3a230a · +6/-0 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3575626 · 4 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

The AI module's OpenAI provider was sending unsupported parameters when using GPT-5.x and o-series models, causing errors that prevented these newer models from working. The provider now automatically filters out unsupported parameters like top_p, temperature, frequency_penalty, and presence_penalty for these model families. This fix allows sites to use the latest GPT models without encountering parameter validation errors.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The OpenAI API has different parameter requirements for different model families. The GPT-5.x and o-series models reject parameters like top_p, temperature, frequency_penalty, and presence_penalty that are accepted by earlier models like GPT-4.

The OpenAiProvider class was sending these parameters regardless of the model being used, which caused API errors when invoking GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, or o-series models. The fix adds logic to detect these newer model families and strip out the unsupported parameters before sending the request.

The detection uses the existing model ID check for o-series models (checking if the model ID starts with 'o') and extends it to cover GPT-5.x models as well. When these models are detected, the provider now explicitly unsets the four unsupported configuration parameters from the request payload. This ensures compatibility across different OpenAI model generations without requiring configuration changes.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: February 25, 2026

Key contributors

  • siva01 (Macron Software, spol. s r.o.)
  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • abhisekmazumdar (Dropsolid)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
